newspaperName: The Fort Worth Record and Register

headlines.title: GENERAL SURVEY OF FIRE
headlines.subtitle: Starts in Barn, Which Itself Was Only Half Burned and Destroyed Beautiful Homes and Churches.

headlines.title: WATER SUFFICIENT-WIND TERRIFIC
headlines.fullText:
Chief Bideker, when seen last night and asked to make a statement about the water supply and the pressure available during the fire, said: "You must remember that the section where the first fire broke out is on the highest point in the city. It would not be reasonable to expect the same pressure there that we could get in the lower parts of the city.
"We had at practically all times ample water supply and good pressure. The worst handicap we had did not come from lack of water pressure, but from lack of hose. The fire was fanhed by a high wind, and it swept from house to house and street to street with almost incredible rapidity. In some instances we lost lines of hose through the sudden sweep of the flames before the lines could be taken to a place of safety.
"I do not care to make a detailed statement until after I have had time to make a thorough investigation of the fire from its various points. It was an especially difficult fire to handle because of the tinder through which it raged. The nature of the buildings, the high wind and the high altitude all tended to increase the difficulties surrounding the handling of the conflagration."
Commissioner Lee Stephens of the water works and sewerage department said in connection with his efforts to increase the supply of water and the force of the water pressure :
"The supply of water in the reservoirs was exhausted before I could hardly snap my fingers, and I succeeded in getting connection with the river at 2 o'clock. The old pumping plant at the river was put into operation, but in order to give increased pressure the old velocity of sixteen revolutions was increased to twenty-eight revolutions, this at the same time giving a capacity of 13,000,000 gallons of water per day. Men were dispatched to the upper dams at the stove foundry to open them up so as to furnish a better supply at the lower dam, from which the water was being drawn."
During the fire the wind blew from the south with a velocity ranging from thirty-two to forty miles an hour. There were occasional variations of intervals ranging from three to five minutes, during which the wind switched to the southwest. The wind was accompanied by a heavy dust, which made it almost unbearable at times.
